nativeRange.habitat: The Striped Glass Catfish is found in peat swamp forests and the blackwater streams associated with them — shaded, slow to moderately flowing waters that are highly acidic and stained amber by organic decay, with dense riparian vegetation and leaf litter along the banks.

decorPreferences: dense planting reaching the water surface
driftwood and twisted roots for structure
dried leaf litter (Indian almond leaves, oak leaves) for tannins

diet.notes: A micropredator in the wild, feeding on small invertebrates, crustaceans, and insect larvae. In the aquarium, most individuals accept frozen and live bloodworm, Tubifex, Daphnia, brine shrimp, and small sinking pellets. Foods must be offered in mid-water suspension, as this species tends not to feed from the surface or the bottom of the tank. A varied diet is recommended; adults may prey on very small tankmates, so housing with nano fish should be avoided.

avoidWith: large or boisterous fish that outcompete for food
very small nano fish (risk of predation by adults)

summary: The Striped Glass Catfish is a larger, more boldly marked relative of the widely kept Glass Catfish (Kryptopterus vitreolus), reaching up to 15 cm and displaying a translucent body with a prominent dark lateral stripe. A schooling species from Southeast Asian peat swamps, it requires soft, acidic blackwater conditions and groups of five or more.
careNotes: The Striped Glass Catfish (Kryptopterus macrocephalus) is a mid-water schooling catfish from the peat swamp forests and blackwater streams of southern Thailand, Peninsular Malaysia, Singapore, Sumatra, Borneo, and Java. It is the larger cousin of the commonly kept Glass Catfish (K. vitreolus), reaching up to 15 cm in length, and is distinguishable by a prominent dark horizontal stripe running along the flank. Like all Kryptopterus catfish, it is scaleless, which makes it highly sensitive to copper-based medications — these must never be used in a tank housing this species. Sudden shifts in water parameters carry a similar risk and should be avoided through careful acclimatisation and consistent maintenance. A school of at least 5 individuals (preferably 6 or more) is essential; solitary or small-group specimens tend to become stressed and refuse food. A tank of at least 160 L with a 120 cm footprint is recommended to give a school adequate swimming space and to support stable water quality. Water conditions should replicate the acidic, very soft blackwater of peat swamps: pH 4.0–7.0, gH 1–10, temperature 20–26°C. Indian almond (Catappa) leaves, peat filtration, or driftwood help achieve the amber-coloured, tannin-rich water the species generally prefers. Dim lighting, provided by floating plants or tall stem plants, is important — this species tends to be shy under bright conditions and will spend much of its time hiding. A moderate, consistent water flow (4–5× tank volume per hour) is appreciated; the fish characteristically positions itself at an angle in the current with its tail pointing down, a resting posture often seen in the genus. It is a mid-water feeder and tends not to scavenge from the substrate, so foods should be offered in suspension — frozen bloodworm, Daphnia, Tubifex, or small sinking pellets that drift down slowly. Tank-raised or long-term captive specimens may accept high-quality frozen and prepared foods more readily than newly imported wild-caught fish. Avoid housing with boisterous, fast-moving, or fin-nipping species that cause stress, and avoid very small tankmates that adult specimens may prey upon. The species is listed as Near Threatened by the IUCN due to peat swamp habitat loss throughout its range and is uncommon in the aquarium trade, typically available only from specialty importers.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity has not been recorded. Sexual dimorphism is subtle: males are noticeably slimmer and have serrated edges on the pectoral-fin spine, while females are deeper-bodied, particularly when gravid. Spawning behaviour and egg care are unknown from captive reports.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Kryptopterus macrocephalus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (~95–100 mm SL), temperature (20–26°C), pH (4.0–7.0), hardness (18–143 ppm ≈ ~1–10 gH), minimum tank dimensions (120 × 30 cm base), diet (sinking/frozen foods, bloodworm, Tubifex), behaviour (schooling, timid, mid-water feeder), native range (S. Thailand, Peninsular Malaysia, Singapore, Sumatra, Borneo, Java), habitat (peat swamp blackwater), breeding (unrecorded in captivity), and sexual dimorphism (male serrated pectoral spine). Minimum group size listed as 4+; bumped to 5 per task brief.

additional.name: FishBase — Kryptopterus macrocephalus
additional.notes: Cross-checked max size (9.7 cm SL males/unsexed), temperature (22–26°C), pH (6.5–7.4), hardness (10–20 dH), native range (Indonesia, Malaysia, Brunei Darussalam), habitat (peat, shady running waters), taxonomy (Siluridae, Siluriformes), diet (omnivore, larger individuals prey on smaller fish), trophic level 4.2. IUCN Near Threatened noted. FishBase pH 6.5–7.4 is a narrower trade range vs. SeriouslyFish wild range 4.0–7.0; used SeriouslyFish broader range as primary.

additional.name: Diszhal.info — Kryptopterus macrocephalus
additional.notes: Confirmed size (9–10 cm), temperature (22–27°C), pH (4–7), hardness (0–10 NK°), minimum tank 90 L (120 L for 5–6 fish), lifespan (6–8 years), diet (omnivore accepting live/frozen/dried), behaviour (peaceful schooling fish), native range (Brunei, Malaysia, Indonesia), breeding (not bred in aquarium). Tail-down resting posture noted.

additional.name: Scotcat — Kryptopterus macrocephalus
additional.notes: Confirmed size (10 cm), temperature (22–26°C), pH (6.5–7.5), native range (Peninsular Thailand, Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, Borneo), habitat (peat habitats, shady running water), characteristic tail-down stationary posture, noted as less peaceful than K. bicirrhis and uncommon in trade.

additional.name: PetMeDaily — Striped Glass Catfish Care
additional.notes: Trade care guide. Confirmed schooling requirement (groups of 6+), temperature 24–28°C, pH 6.0–7.5, tank 20 gal+, diet (frozen bloodworm, brine shrimp, tropical flakes, sinking pellets), compatible tankmates (small tetras, rasboras, Corydoras), copper/water chemistry sensitivity noted, escape risk noted (tank cover required). Adult size cited as ~4 inches.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive breeding on record. Males are slimmer with serrated pectoral-fin spines; females are deeper-bodied when gravid. Spawning triggers and egg care are entirely unknown from aquarium reports.
